claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have a child - my son Alex, he's 8 years old and lives here at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex.

Now, to assess your eligibility properly, could you please tell me: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're based in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. So to confirm, you live in the UK and Alex lives with you at home.

Let me ask: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a few more questions to ensure I have all the information I need.

**Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation, or has he been in any of these situations for an extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for about three months.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I understand Alex has been in hospital for about three months.

To help me assess your eligibility correctly, could you please tell me: **Do you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital - for example, on things like clothes, treats, toys, or other items for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I still buy things for him - clothes, magazines, and his favorite snacks and treats when I visit. I make sure he has what he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's very helpful.

Just to make sure I have all the details I need: **Are there any other children you're responsible for or would like to inquire about for Child Benefit purposes?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it's just Alex. He's my only child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex based on what you've told me.

Based on the information you've provided:

- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- Alex is 8 years old and normally lives with you at home
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (12 weeks)
- You regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital (clothes, magazines, snacks, and treats)

According to the official guidance, when a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks, Child Benefit payments might normally stop. However, there is an important exception: you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on the child while they're in hospital.

Since you are regularly spending money on Alex by buying him clothes, magazines, snacks, and treats during his hospital stay, you meet the exception to the 12-week rule.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Even though Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (which is around the 12-week mark where entitlement can be affected), you qualify under an important exception. Because you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital - buying him clothes, magazines, snacks, and treats - you can continue to receive Child Benefit.

You meet all the standard eligibility criteria:
- You live in the UK
- Alex is under 16 (he's 8 years old)
- You're responsible for him
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him

Since Alex has been in hospital for this length of time, it would be a good idea to inform HMRC about his hospital stay if you haven't already done so, and let them know that you're regularly spending money on him. This will help ensure your payments continue without interruption.

I hope Alex recovers well and is home soon!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives in the UK with the claimant when not in hospital, and the claimant is responsible for him. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), which would normally affect Child Benefit entitlement, the claimant regularly spends money on Alex during his hospital stay (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, and treats). According to the rules, an exception applies for children in UK hospitals when the claimant regularly spends money on them, allowing Child Benefit to continue beyond the 12-week limit. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their only child, Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for approximately 12 weeks (which would normally affect entitlement), the claimant qualifies under the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital. All other eligibility criteria are met: the claimant lives in the UK, Alex is under 16, and no one else is claiming for him.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives in the UK with the claimant when not in hospital, and the claimant is responsible for him. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), which would normally affect Child Benefit entitlement, the claimant regularly spends money on Alex during his hospital stay (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, and treats). According to the rules, an exception applies for children in UK hospitals when the claimant regularly spends money on them, allowing Child Benefit to continue beyond the 12-week limit. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their only child, Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for approximately 12 weeks (which would normally affect entitlement), the claimant qualifies under the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital. All other eligibility criteria are met: the claimant lives in the UK, Alex is under 16, and no one else is claiming for him.
